title: The Benefits of Volunteering
summary: A narrator explores how volunteering benefits not only communities and organisations but also the volunteers themselves, improving their skills, wellbeing, and sense of purpose.
body: Volunteering means giving your time, energy, or skills to help others without expecting payment. People volunteer in many different ways: some work in food banks, community gardens, or local libraries; others mentor young people, visit elderly residents, or support those dealing with illness. Whatever form it takes, volunteering is one of the most powerful ways to make a positive contribution to society. Most people think of volunteering primarily in terms of what it gives to others — and it certainly does. Food banks keep families from going hungry. Mentoring programmes give young people access to guidance they might not otherwise receive. Hospice volunteers offer comfort to patients and families during difficult times. The positive impact on communities is real, measurable, and often profound. What is less often discussed, however, is what volunteering gives to the volunteer. Research consistently shows that people who volunteer regularly report higher levels of life satisfaction, lower rates of depression, and a stronger sense of purpose. This may seem surprising at first. Surely spending your free time helping others is tiring, not energising. But psychological studies suggest that the act of helping releases feel-good chemicals in the brain, creating what researchers sometimes call a helper's high. This natural reward makes the activity feel meaningful rather than burdensome. Volunteering is also an excellent way to develop professional skills. Young people who volunteer often discover abilities they did not know they had — organisational skills, leadership, communication, and the capacity to work calmly under pressure. For those returning to work after a career break, volunteering can help rebuild confidence and update skills that may have become rusty. Even experienced professionals can gain fresh perspectives by working in environments very different from their usual workplace. There are social benefits too. Volunteering brings people together who might never otherwise meet. A retired teacher, a university student, and a young parent might all work side by side at a community event, building connections across age groups, backgrounds, and experiences. These connections can reduce loneliness and strengthen community bonds in ways that formal programmes rarely manage to achieve. Of course, volunteering is not without challenges. It requires a time commitment that not everyone can make. People with demanding jobs or caring responsibilities may simply not have the capacity. And some organisations do not always manage their volunteers well, which can lead to frustration. But for those who do have the time and find the right role, volunteering offers a rare combination: you give something valuable, and you receive something valuable in return.

annotations.meaningVi: ngân hàng thực phẩm (nơi phân phát thức ăn miễn phí)
annotations.exampleEn: Thousands of families rely on food banks during periods of financial hardship.

annotations.meaningVi: hướng dẫn, làm người cố vấn cho ai
annotations.exampleEn: She volunteered to mentor young women entering the technology industry.
Everyone can make a positive contribution to their local community.

annotations.meaningVi: sâu sắc, to lớn, rất đáng kể
annotations.exampleEn: The experience of living abroad had a profound effect on her outlook.

annotations.meaningVi: sự hài lòng với cuộc sống
annotations.exampleEn: Studies link regular exercise to higher levels of life satisfaction.

annotations.meaningVi: cảm giác có mục đích sống
annotations.exampleEn: Many retirees find a stronger sense of purpose through volunteering.

annotations.meaningVi: các chất hóa học tạo cảm giác dễ chịu trong não (như endorphin)
annotations.exampleEn: Exercise releases feel-good chemicals that improve mood naturally.

annotations.meaningVi: cảm giác hưng phấn khi giúp đỡ người khác
annotations.exampleEn: Researchers have studied the 'helper's high' — the boost in wellbeing that comes from helping others.
The extra workload felt burdensome without enough support.

annotations.meaningVi: kỳ nghỉ nghề nghiệp, thời gian tạm dừng công việc
annotations.exampleEn: She took a career break to care for her children and returned to work after three years.

annotations.meaningVi: trở nên mai một, mất đi sự thành thạo
annotations.exampleEn: His French had become rusty after years of not using it.

annotations.meaningVi: góc nhìn mới mẻ, cách nhìn mới
annotations.exampleEn: Working abroad gave her fresh perspectives on her own culture.
People from very different backgrounds worked side by side at the community garden.
Local events help strengthen community bonds between neighbours.

annotations.meaningVi: cam kết thời gian, sự đầu tư thời gian
annotations.exampleEn: Regular volunteering requires a serious time commitment each week.

annotations.meaningVi: trách nhiệm chăm sóc (ví dụ: chăm con hoặc người thân)
annotations.exampleEn: Many parents find it hard to volunteer due to their caring responsibilities.

grammarNotes.title: Adverbs of Frequency and Certainty — 'consistently', 'regularly', 'rarely', 'simply'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài sử dụng nhiều trạng từ tần suất và mức độ chắc chắn để nhấn mạnh các xu hướng và sự kiện: 'Research consistently shows', 'people who volunteer regularly report', 'formal programmes rarely manage to achieve', 'may simply not have the capacity'. Các trạng từ này thường đứng trước động từ chính hoặc giữa trợ động từ và động từ chính. Chúng giúp bài văn học thuật nghe chính xác và đáng tin cậy hơn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses adverbs of frequency and certainty to highlight trends and claims: 'Research consistently shows', 'people who volunteer regularly report', 'formal programmes rarely manage to achieve', 'may simply not have the capacity'. These adverbs usually go before the main verb or between the auxiliary and main verb. They make academic writing sound precise and credible.

grammarNotes.title: Contrast Connectors — 'however', 'but', 'of course … but', 'rather than'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng nhiều từ nối tương phản để cân bằng các lập luận: 'What is less often discussed, however, is...', 'Surely spending your free time helping others is tiring, not energising. But psychological studies suggest...', 'Of course, volunteering is not without challenges... But for those who do...'. Cấu trúc 'rather than' cũng xuất hiện: 'feel meaningful rather than burdensome'. Các từ nối này giúp bài viết trình bày cả hai mặt của vấn đề một cách cân bằng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses contrast connectors to balance arguments: 'What is less often discussed, however, is...', 'Surely spending your free time helping others is tiring, not energising. But psychological studies suggest...', 'Of course, volunteering is not without challenges... But for those who do...'. The structure 'rather than' also appears: 'feel meaningful rather than burdensome'. These connectors help the writing present both sides of an issue fairly.

translationVi:
Tình nguyện có nghĩa là dành thời gian, năng lượng hoặc kỹ năng của bạn để giúp đỡ người khác mà không kỳ vọng được trả tiền. Mọi người tình nguyện theo nhiều cách khác nhau: một số làm việc tại ngân hàng thực phẩm, vườn cộng đồng hoặc thư viện địa phương; những người khác hướng dẫn giới trẻ, thăm viếng người cao tuổi, hoặc hỗ trợ những người đang đối mặt với bệnh tật. Dù hình thức nào, tình nguyện là một trong những cách mạnh mẽ nhất để đóng góp tích cực cho xã hội.

Hầu hết mọi người chủ yếu nghĩ đến tình nguyện theo nghĩa những gì nó mang lại cho người khác — và điều đó chắc chắn đúng. Ngân hàng thực phẩm giúp các gia đình không bị đói. Các chương trình hướng dẫn cho phép thanh thiếu niên tiếp cận sự dẫn dắt mà họ có thể không nhận được theo cách khác. Các tình nguyện viên chăm sóc giảm nhẹ mang lại sự an ủi cho bệnh nhân và gia đình trong những giai đoạn khó khăn. Tác động tích cực đến cộng đồng là thực sự, có thể đo lường được và thường rất sâu sắc.

Tuy nhiên, điều ít được thảo luận hơn là những gì tình nguyện mang lại cho chính người tình nguyện. Nghiên cứu liên tục cho thấy những người tình nguyện thường xuyên báo cáo mức độ hài lòng với cuộc sống cao hơn, tỷ lệ trầm cảm thấp hơn và cảm giác có mục đích mạnh mẽ hơn. Điều này có vẻ ngạc nhiên lúc đầu. Chắc chắn dành thời gian rảnh giúp đỡ người khác thì mệt mỏi chứ không phải tràn đầy năng lượng. Nhưng các nghiên cứu tâm lý cho thấy hành động giúp đỡ giải phóng các hóa chất tạo cảm giác dễ chịu trong não, tạo ra điều mà các nhà nghiên cứu đôi khi gọi là 'cảm giác hưng phấn của người giúp đỡ'. Phần thưởng tự nhiên này khiến hoạt động cảm thấy có ý nghĩa thay vì nặng nề.

Tình nguyện cũng là một cách tuyệt vời để phát triển kỹ năng chuyên nghiệp. Những người trẻ tình nguyện thường khám phá ra những khả năng mà họ không biết mình có — kỹ năng tổ chức, lãnh đạo, giao tiếp và khả năng làm việc bình tĩnh dưới áp lực. Đối với những người trở lại làm việc sau kỳ nghỉ nghề nghiệp, tình nguyện có thể giúp xây dựng lại sự tự tin và cập nhật các kỹ năng có thể đã mai một. Ngay cả các chuyên gia có kinh nghiệm cũng có thể có được góc nhìn mới bằng cách làm việc trong các môi trường rất khác với nơi làm việc thông thường của họ.

Ngoài ra còn có những lợi ích xã hội. Tình nguyện gắn kết những người có thể không bao giờ gặp nhau theo cách khác. Một giáo viên đã về hưu, một sinh viên đại học và một phụ huynh trẻ có thể làm việc cạnh nhau tại một sự kiện cộng đồng, xây dựng các mối liên kết xuyên qua các nhóm tuổi, xuất thân và kinh nghiệm. Những mối liên kết này có thể giảm bớt sự cô đơn và tăng cường các mối liên kết cộng đồng theo những cách mà các chương trình chính thức hiếm khi đạt được.

Tất nhiên, tình nguyện không phải là không có thách thức. Nó đòi hỏi cam kết về thời gian mà không phải ai cũng có thể thực hiện. Những người có công việc đòi hỏi cao hoặc trách nhiệm chăm sóc người thân có thể đơn giản là không có khả năng. Và một số tổ chức không luôn quản lý tình nguyện viên tốt, điều này có thể dẫn đến sự thất vọng. Nhưng đối với những người có thời gian và tìm được đúng vai trò, tình nguyện cung cấp một sự kết hợp hiếm có: bạn cho đi thứ gì đó có giá trị, và nhận lại thứ gì đó có giá trị.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what does volunteering mean?
options: Working part-time for a reduced salary
Giving your time, energy, or skills to help others without expecting payment
Joining community events organised by the government
questions.explanation: The passage defines it as 'giving your time, energy, or skills to help others without expecting payment.'
questions.hint: The passage gives a direct definition in the first sentence.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is NOT mentioned in the passage as a form of volunteering?
questions.explanation: Teaching foreign languages online is not mentioned. The passage lists food banks, mentoring, visiting elderly residents, and hospice work.
questions.hint: The passage gives specific examples in the second sentence.

questions.prompt: What benefit do hospice volunteers provide, according to the passage?
options: Comfort to patients and families during difficult times
Transportation to and from hospital appointments
questions.explanation: The passage says 'Hospice volunteers offer comfort to patients and families during difficult times.'
questions.hint: The passage focuses on emotional support, not practical services.

questions.prompt: What does research show about people who volunteer regularly?
options: They earn more money over the course of their careers
They live significantly longer than non-volunteers
They report higher life satisfaction, lower depression, and a stronger sense of purpose
They develop better physical health from the activity
questions.explanation: The passage states that volunteers 'report higher levels of life satisfaction, lower rates of depression, and a stronger sense of purpose.'
questions.hint: The passage lists three specific psychological benefits.

options: A feeling of exhaustion after a long volunteer shift
A boost in wellbeing caused by the release of feel-good chemicals when helping others
The pride of being recognised for volunteer work
A state of flow achieved during skilled tasks
questions.explanation: The passage explains it as 'feel-good chemicals in the brain' released by the act of helping, creating a natural reward.
questions.hint: The term is introduced after the mention of brain chemicals.

questions.prompt: What professional skills can young people discover through volunteering?
options: Technical skills specific to their chosen career
Organisational skills, leadership, communication, and working under pressure
Digital marketing and social media skills
questions.explanation: The passage lists 'organisational skills, leadership, communication, and the capacity to work calmly under pressure.'
questions.hint: The passage names four transferable skills.

questions.prompt: How can volunteering help people returning to work after a career break?
options: It provides a reference letter from a well-known organisation
It pays a small wage that helps during the transition
It can rebuild confidence and update skills that have become rusty
It connects them directly with employers looking for staff
questions.explanation: The passage says volunteering 'can help rebuild confidence and update skills that may have become rusty.'
questions.hint: The passage uses the word 'rusty' as a metaphor.

questions.prompt: What social benefit of volunteering does the passage highlight?
options: It creates formal networks of professionals in the same field
It brings together people from different ages and backgrounds who might never otherwise meet
It reduces crime rates in areas with many volunteers
It gives volunteers access to exclusive social events
questions.explanation: The passage says volunteering 'brings people together who might never otherwise meet' and describes connections across 'age groups, backgrounds, and experiences.'
questions.hint: The passage gives an example with three very different types of people working together.

questions.prompt: What does the passage say these social connections can reduce?
questions.explanation: The passage says these connections 'can reduce loneliness and strengthen community bonds.'
questions.hint: The passage mentions two results; one relates to isolation.

questions.prompt: What challenge of volunteering does the passage acknowledge?
options: Volunteers are often not treated as equal to paid staff
It requires a time commitment not everyone can make
Volunteer organisations often lack meaningful work
questions.explanation: The passage says 'It requires a time commitment that not everyone can make.'
questions.hint: The passage identifies one practical barrier to volunteering.

questions.prompt: How does the passage conclude its argument about volunteering?
options: That the difficulties outweigh the benefits for most people
That governments should make volunteering compulsory
That for those who can do it, volunteering is a rare opportunity where you both give and receive something valuable
That online volunteering is the best solution for busy people
questions.explanation: The passage ends: 'you give something valuable, and you receive something valuable in return.'
questions.hint: The final sentence summarises the mutual benefit of volunteering.

cloze.template: Volunteering means giving your time, energy, or skills to help others without expecting {{b1}}. People volunteer in many different ways: some work in food banks, community gardens, or local libraries; others {{b2}} young people, visit elderly residents, or support those dealing with illness. Whatever form it takes, volunteering is one of the most powerful ways to make a positive {{b3}} to society. Most people think of volunteering primarily in terms of what it gives to others. Food banks keep families from going {{b4}}. Hospice volunteers offer comfort to patients and families during difficult times. The positive impact on communities is real, measurable, and often {{b5}}. Research {{b6}} shows that people who volunteer regularly report higher levels of life satisfaction and a stronger sense of purpose. Psychological studies suggest that the act of helping releases feel-good chemicals in the brain, creating what researchers sometimes call a helper's high. This natural reward makes the activity feel meaningful rather than {{b7}}. Volunteering is also an excellent way to develop professional skills. Young people who volunteer often discover abilities they did not know they had — {{b8}} skills, leadership, communication, and the capacity to work calmly under pressure. For those returning to work after a career break, volunteering can help rebuild confidence and update skills that may have become {{b9}}. Volunteering brings people together who might never otherwise meet, building connections across age groups, backgrounds, and experiences. These connections can reduce loneliness and strengthen community {{b10}} in ways that formal programmes rarely manage to achieve.

blanks.explanation: 'Without expecting payment' — 'payment' is the exact word used, the most direct way to say compensation for work.
'Mentor young people' is the verb used — to mentor means to act as an experienced guide or adviser to someone less experienced.
'Positive contribution to society' is the phrase — 'contribution' collocates with 'make a' and 'to society' in this context.
'Keep families from going hungry' — this is a common expression about food insecurity and the purpose of food banks.
'Often profound' — 'profound' means deeply significant or far-reaching, and is used here to describe the depth of impact.
'Research consistently shows' — 'consistently' means that the finding appears reliably across many studies, making it a strong claim.
'Feel meaningful rather than burdensome' — 'burdensome' means something that is heavy, demanding, or an unwanted obligation.
'Organisational skills' is the exact phrase from the passage — it refers to the ability to arrange activities, resources, and people effectively.
'Skills that may have become rusty' — 'rusty' is a metaphor for skills that have deteriorated through lack of use, like metal that rusts when unused.
'Community bonds' is the collocation used — 'bonds' suggests strong, lasting connections between people.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The passage argues that volunteering benefits the volunteer as much as the people being helped. Do you think people should volunteer because it is good for them personally, or should the motivation be purely to help others? Does the reason for volunteering matter?
